title: State Rep. Chris Kurka to File Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Alaska
author: James Silberman
orientation: Free the States announces that Alaska Rep. Chris Kurka, with David Eastman as cosponsor, is filing the Preborn Child Equality Act of 2021 to totally and immediately abolish abortion in Alaska by extending equal protection to preborn children, stripping the State Supreme Court's claimed jurisdiction over the issue, and authorizing defiance of federal court orders that violate the right to life.

questions.question: What does the Preborn Child Equality Act of 2021 actually do, and how is it different from a typical pro-life regulation?
questions.answer: It does not regulate abortion; it abolishes it by redefining personhood from conception and extending equal protection of the laws (including homicide statutes) to preborn children in Alaska.
questions.quote: The Preborn Child Equality Act of 2021 is set to be filed by Alaska State Rep. Christopher Kurka in the coming days. The bill would totally and immediately abolish abortion in Alaska.

questions.question: How does the bill define a 'natural person,' and why is that definition foundational to the equal-protection strategy?
questions.answer: It defines a natural person as a human being from conception, deliberately stripping out every common pretext (age, location, dependency, stage of development) used to deny personhood to the preborn so that existing laws against murder must apply equally.
questions.quote: Kurka's bill defines a "natural person" as "a human being, regardless of age, race, religion, size, sex, citizenship, parentage, ancestry, disability, deformity, location, stage of development, life expectancy, or condition of dependency from the moment of conception."

questions.question: How does the bill establish state residency for preborn children, and why does that matter?
questions.answer: It ties the preborn child's residency to the mother's, ensuring Alaska's jurisdiction and laws (including the protection against being murdered) attach to that child as a resident of the state.
questions.quote: It also establishes that "A preborn child is a resident of the state if the mother of the preborn child is a resident of the state under this section."

questions.question: Why is the bill targeting the Alaska Supreme Court, not just abortion providers?
questions.answer: Because the Alaska Supreme Court, mirroring Roe, judicially manufactured a state constitutional 'right' to abortion under privacy; abolishing abortion in statute requires repudiating that judicial fiction.
questions.quote: Just as was done federally in *Roe v. Wade*, the Alaska Supreme Court wrote a right to abortion into the State Constitution by arguing that murdering a preborn child is a protected right within the State Constitution's right to privacy.

questions.question: How does the bill attempt to remove abortion from judicial review?
questions.answer: It explicitly declares that intentionally killing a natural person before, during, or after birth is not protected by Alaska's constitutional right to privacy and that this section is not subject to judicial review.
questions.quote: Notwithstanding any other provision of law, intentionally causing the death of a natural person before, during, or after birth is not protected by a right to privacy under art. I, sec. 22, Constitution of the State of Alaska. This section is not subject to judicial review.

questions.question: Isn't it improper for the legislature to put a topic off-limits to the courts?
questions.answer: Free the States argues no, because Alaska's own constitution gives the legislature the power to prescribe court jurisdiction, providing a deliberate check on judicial overreach.
questions.quote: Some object to the idea that the legislature may declare certain topics off limits for the judiciary, but article IV, section 1 of the Alaska Constitution states that "The jurisdiction of courts shall be prescribed by law."

questions.question: How does the bill propose to handle conflicting federal court orders?
questions.answer: It expands an existing Alaska nullification statute (44.99.040) to add federal court orders to the list of federal actions Alaska may treat as null and void, and it adds the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendment right to life to the protected rights triggering that defiance.
questions.quote: Kurka's bill adds federal court orders to the list of federal actions which may be defied, and adds the "right to life under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution of the United States" to the list of rights which Alaska will defy the federal government to protect.

questions.question: What does Free the States ask supporters to do in response to this bill?
questions.answer: Pray for Kurka, Eastman, and the bill, and follow Free the States' blog and Facebook page for ongoing updates on Alaska and other state abolition bills.
questions.quote: Free the States thanks Representatives Kurka and Eastman for their bold stand. Be in prayer for them and for the Preborn Child Equality of Act of 2021.

questions.question: Why should an Alaska voter care that David Eastman is cosponsoring rather than authoring this round?
questions.answer: Eastman is the veteran abolition-bill author in Alaska; his cosponsorship signals continuity with prior abolition efforts while Kurka steps forward as the new lead author, broadening the legislative coalition for total abolition.
questions.quote: State Rep. David Eastman, who has authored abolition bills in the past, will be a cosponsor.

related.title: Alaska State Reps. Introduce Abortion Abolition Bill
related.reason: Direct predecessor: the 2019 Alaska abolition bill that David Eastman authored, which Kurka's 2021 bill builds on.

related.title: Alaska Abolition Bill Author David Eastman Wins Primary
related.reason: Background on the cosponsor and the political conditions in Alaska that made the 2021 Preborn Child Equality Act possible.

related.title: State Rep. Bryan Slaton Files Bill to Immediately Abolish Abortion in Texas
related.reason: Parallel 2021 state abolition bill using the same equal-protection-from-conception framework.

related.title: Bill to Abolish Abortion in South Carolina Filed by State Rep. Jonathan Hill
related.reason: Another 2021 sister bill in the multi-state push for immediate abolition rather than incremental regulation.

related.title: Reps. Curt Nisly, John Jacob Author the Bill to Abolish Abortion in Indiana
related.reason: Same legislative model in Indiana, illustrating the coordinated state-by-state strategy of which Alaska's bill is part.

related.title: Abortion Abolition Bill Introduced in Washington
related.reason: An earlier West-Coast abolition bill on similar theory, useful for comparison to Alaska's approach.

related.title: First Ever Bill to Ignore Roe and Abolish Abortion in Colorado: HB22-1079
related.reason: Follow-on Colorado bill that, like Kurka's, explicitly defies federal authority on abortion.
